_comment: Shelter & disease-control protocol for incident_type=shelter_disease. Population-level operational guidance for trained responders, paraphrased from WHO/PAHO emergency guidance and Sphere shelter standards. No individual diagnosis or treatment - case identification here is shelter-level surveillance and cohorting only. Loaded by matcher.py at startup; keywords drive the forgiving fallback match on needs/notes.
title: Shelter crowding and communicable-disease control

guidance.action: Make measles vaccination of children (typically 6 months to 15 years) a first-line priority at shelter intake, run jointly with the responsible health authority.
guidance.rationale: Measles spreads explosively in crowded, low-coverage shelters and is a leading cause of preventable outbreak deaths after disasters.

guidance.action: Reduce crowding toward at least about 3.5 m2 of covered living space per person, keep sleeping areas grouped by family, and ventilate enclosed spaces.
guidance.rationale: Crowding is the main amplifier of measles and other airborne transmission inside shelters.

guidance.action: Set up a separated cohort area for suspected fever-and-rash or acute watery diarrhoea cases, away from communal sleeping, pending referral to health services.
guidance.rationale: Early shelter-level separation slows transmission before health authorities confirm what is circulating.

guidance.action: Cut faecal-oral spread inside the shelter: safe drinking water, soap and handwashing points, rapid safe management of excreta, and clean food-preparation surfaces.
guidance.rationale: Diarrhoeal disease and cholera are the shelter's second major outbreak threat after measles.

guidance.action: Run a simple daily surveillance tally - counts of fever-and-rash and of acute watery diarrhoea - and escalate to the health authority on any suspected measles case or a sudden rise in diarrhoea counts.
guidance.rationale: A paper tally is enough to detect an outbreak days earlier, while it is still containable.

guidance.action: Manage the dead with dignity through the responsible authority; bodies of disaster trauma victims are not in themselves an epidemic source, so do not divert outbreak resources to rushed mass burials.
guidance.rationale: The corpse-epidemic belief is a documented post-disaster myth that wastes control resources and harms families.

safety_flags: Coordinate vaccination and outbreak response with the responsible health authority
Population-level disease control only - refer individual care to health services
Escalate any suspected measles case to the health authority immediately
